heading: Mixed Final Column
body: A **contingency** is a formula whose main operator column contains both T and F. The formula is true in some rows and false in others.

examples.text: If the final column is `T F T T`, the formula is a contingency.
If the final column is `T T T T`, the formula is a contingency.

note: The classification depends on the final column only.

heading: Check The Mix
body: To classify a formula as a contingency, inspect the main operator column after the truth table is complete. There must be at least one T and at least one F.

examples.text: `T F T F` passes the test.
`F F F F` passes the test.

heading: Not Always One Value
body: A contingency is neither always true nor always false. Its truth depends on which row, and therefore which assignment of base variables, is being considered.

examples.text: A formula with final column `T T T T` is always true, so it is not a contingency.
A formula with final column `F F F F` is always false, so it is not a contingency.

heading: Simple Cases
body: Read only the final column when classifying the whole statement.

examples.text: Final column `T F T T` means contingency.
Final column `F T F T` means contingency.
Final column `T T T T` means contingency.

heading: Do Not Guess
body: A common mistake is calling any nonuniform-looking formula a contingency before checking the final column. Only the main operator column determines the classification.

examples.text: Inner columns may vary even when the final column is all T or all F.
If any inner column has both T and F, the whole formula must be a contingency.

note: Check the whole formula, not one part of it.

heading: Core Test
body: Complete the truth table, then inspect the main operator column. If it contains both T and F, the formula is a contingency.
